title: Abolitionist Theological Foundations
orientation: Abolitionists Rising's pillar statement on the theology that grounds the movement: abortion is the sin of murder, the gospel of Christ's kingdom is the only ultimate cure, the Church is uniquely entrusted with that cure, Scripture is the sole standard for action, and results are God's prerogative.
audiences: Christian considering whether to join the abolitionist movement
Pastor weighing how the church should respond to abortion
Skeptic or unbeliever asking why this is framed religiously
Lawmaker or political strategist questioning unpopular positions

questions.question: How does abolitionism's view of abortion differ from the standard pro-life position?
questions.answer: Abolitionists go beyond calling abortion 'bad' to identifying it as the sin of murder, which means it must be treated with the moral weight and legal response due to murder, while still holding out forgiveness in Christ.
questions.quote: We, as an abolitionist organizations, agree with the pro-life position that abortion is bad, but further, we believe that abortion is the sin of murder and that it must be treated as such. Of course, there is forgiveness to be found in Christ for abortion, but that does not mean it ought to be legal, and it does not negate the necessity of establishing justice.

questions.question: Why does calling abortion a 'sin' matter strategically for how the movement fights it?
questions.audience: Christian considering whether to join the abolitionist movement
questions.answer: If abortion is fundamentally sin, then the only sufficient remedy is the gospel of Jesus Christ, because Scripture consistently shows that human strength and schemes cannot push back darkness; only God does that, often through His people.
questions.quote: The most important word in the previous paragraph is "sin." Abortion being a sin has huge implications for how we must fight it. That it's a sin means that the only ultimate answer to it is the gospel of Jesus Christ. There is hardly a more common theme of the scriptures than that of man's strength and schemes being impotent to push back darkness. It is always and only God who works -- often through His people -- to overcome evil.

questions.question: What does the article mean by 'the gospel of the kingdom,' and why is that broader than personal salvation?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing how the church should respond to abortion
questions.answer: The gospel includes the forgiveness of sins, but it also includes Christ's reign, judgment, and teaching; Mark calls his entire account 'the gospel,' and Paul ties the gospel to Christ destroying every rule and authority and reigning until His enemies are under His feet.
questions.quote: In 1 Corinthians, Paul writes of the crucifixion and resurrection of Christ, but he then turns to the reign of Christ - the gospel of the kingdom. He quotes from Psalm 110, saying, “Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power. For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death.”

questions.question: How do abolitionists understand their public activism in relation to the kingdom of God?
questions.audience: Christian considering whether to join the abolitionist movement
questions.answer: Abolitionists see themselves as bringing Christ's kingdom of justice and mercy into direct conflict with the world's kingdom of sin and death, advancing the kingdom as souls are redeemed and darkness is pushed back.
questions.quote: It is this gospel of Christ's kingdom that abolitionists bring into conflict with the culture of death. We proclaim the excellency of Christ's kingdom of justice and mercy over against the world's kingdom of sin and death. We proclaim God's wisdom against the foolishness of man. We proclaim repentance and belief in the One who died to make us right with God, and who will one day set everything right.

questions.question: Why is the abolition movement described as inescapably Christian rather than a broad secular coalition?
questions.audience: Skeptic or unbeliever asking why this is framed religiously
questions.answer: Because the cure for sin is the gospel, and the gospel has been entrusted only to the Church; unbelievers operating with common grace can support just laws, but only the Church possesses the actual remedy for the curse that produces abortion.
questions.quote: So if abortion is sin, and the answer to sin is the gospel, then whose work is it to push back the darkness of abortion? It can only be those to whom the gospel has been entrusted: the Church (Matthew 28:19-20). While unbelievers operating with common grace are welcome to support just laws and candidates, this movement is unavoidably a Christian one. No one else has the cure to the curse that plagues mankind and leads to abortion.

questions.question: Is fighting abortion an optional ministry for specially gifted Christians, or a duty for the whole Church?
questions.audience: Pastor weighing how the church should respond to abortion
questions.answer: It is a universal duty for the whole Church, not a niche calling; the parable of the Good Samaritan shows that ignoring a neighbor in need is sin that transcends individual gifts, and Christ rebuked the Pharisees for neglecting justice.
questions.quote: This work does not belong to a special few within the Church, but the Church as a whole. As R.C. Sproul put it, “When the church is silent in the midst of a holocaust, she ceases to be a real church.” Whether one should work to stop a holocaust that is all around them is not a matter of Christian liberty. In the parable of the good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37), Jesus demonstrates that religious people will often pass by a neighbor in need, and that this is an example of sin — the neglect of a universal duty that transcends any particular gifts, skills, or callings that individual Christians may have.

questions.question: If a state has 'banned' abortion, can Christians there consider the work finished?
questions.audience: Christian considering whether to join the abolitionist movement
questions.answer: No. Even where surgical abortion mills have closed, mothers still kill their children with mail-order pills under cover of law, and pharmacies, OBGYN offices, and websites continue to facilitate abortion; the Church must keep engaging the culture and exposing the evil.
questions.quote: In the modern American context, there is almost no one who does not pass by the places of murder on a daily basis, and if you live in a state where abortion has supposedly been made "illegal", mothers are still murdering their babies with mail-order pills under the cover of law. If there is not an abortion mill in your area, there is a hospital or OBGYN office that performs abortions, a pharmacy that sells abortifacient drugs, or internet access to sites that sell abortion pills. Child sacrifice is all around us.

questions.question: What is the abolitionist standard for what laws and proposals to support?
questions.audience: Lawmaker or political strategist questioning unpopular positions
questions.answer: Scripture alone. If Christ and the prophets demanded immediate repentance and refused to endorse partial bans on injustice, abolitionists must do likewise; they will not support legislation that bans abortion after a certain week while permitting it before.
questions.quote: If Christ and the prophets demanded immediate repentance of the kings and nations, so too must we. If Jesus and the prophets called the magistrates to establish justice, so too must we. If Jesus and the prophets would not support legislation banning abortion after 15 weeks but allowing it previously, then neither can we.

questions.question: How does the article critique pro-life leaders who keep their faith out of their activism?
questions.answer: By excluding God's Word, pro-life leaders forfeit the only objective standard by which abortion can be adequately rebuked as evil; the article cites a pro-life outreach that forbade volunteers from evangelizing and a pro-life senator who said he leaves his Christianity at the door.
questions.quote: Before abolitionists convinced them to stop, one pro-life outreach organization made their volunteers sign a form promising not to evangelize unless the unbeliever brings up religion first. A pro-life Oklahoma State Senator told a constituent that he leaves his Christianity at the door when he’s legislating. The pro-life leaders believe they must downplay their Christianity, and the result is that they have given up the only objective standard by which abortion can be adequately rebuked as evil.

questions.question: How do abolitionists answer the charge that their positions are politically unrealistic or don't poll well?
questions.audience: Lawmaker or political strategist questioning unpopular positions
questions.answer: They reject pragmatism as the standard. Faithfulness to God's commands is their duty; results belong to God, who alone opens eyes, softens hearts, and raises up or brings down rulers.
questions.quote: Accordingly, abolitionists take our marching orders from God’s Word, not from secularist political experts who kick against abolitionism because many of our positions don’t poll well in the current political climate. But abolitionists don’t care. God’s prescriptions are not only for applying in favorable environments. We are to call for justice as God defines it in faith that He is the one who opens eyes, softens hearts, sets up and brings down kings, and vanquishes the darkness.

questions.question: What role does faith play in actually overcoming abortion, according to the article?
questions.audience: Skeptic or unbeliever asking why this is framed religiously
questions.answer: Because abortion is spiritual, the victory over it is spiritual: 1 John 5 teaches that the victory that overcomes the world is faith in Jesus as the Son of God, so the destruction of abortion cannot be detached from belief in God's Word, promises, and prescriptions.
questions.quote: 1 John 5:4-5 teach us, “For everyone who has been born of God overcomes the world. And this is the victory that has overcome the world—our faith. Who is it that overcomes the world except the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God?” The overcoming of abortion cannot be detached from its spiritual nature and implications. Faith in God, His Word, His promises, and His prescriptions are crucial to the destruction of abortion.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: The article explicitly points to Immediatism as the next pillar page; it applies the theological refusal to support partial bans laid out here.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Named as the follow-on pillar covering Criminalization/Equal Protection, working out the 'abortion is murder' premise in law.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Corresponds to the 'Defy Tyrants' pillar the article promises next, applying these foundations to the magistrate's duty.

related.title: Biblical, Not Secular
related.reason: Directly parallels the article's sola scriptura argument that Scripture, not secular pragmatism, must govern the movement.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Expands the article's contrast between abolitionism and pro-life leaders who downplay Christianity and accept compromise.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Develops the gospel-and-repentance theme that this page identifies as the only ultimate cure for abortion.

related.title: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Embodies the 'duty is ours, results belong to God' posture that closes this theological foundation.
